dyed_lifted_polyps: Unlike regular polyps, these lesions are both lifted and stained, making their borders and surface patterns more distinct.
The combination of submucosal injection and dye application differentiates dyed lifted polyps from unstained or flat lesions.
Dyed lifted polyps are easier to distinguish from surrounding mucosa due to enhanced contrast.
The raised and colored appearance sets them apart from non-lifted or non-dyed polyps.
This class is specifically identified by the use of chromoendoscopy and submucosal lifting techniques.
dyed_resection_margins: These images focus on the margins after polyp removal, highlighted by dye, unlike intact polyps or normal mucosa.
The presence of dye at the resection site distinguishes this class from other post-procedural or untreated areas.
Resection margins are evaluated for completeness of excision, not for the presence of new lesions.
Unlike dyed lifted polyps, the main feature here is the assessment of the excision boundary.
This class is unique in showing the interface between treated and untreated tissue after intervention.
esophagitis: Characterized by inflamed, erythematous, or ulcerated esophageal mucosa, unlike the normal Z-line or healthy tissue.
Esophagitis images show pathological changes absent in normal anatomical classes.
The presence of erosions, swelling, or exudates differentiates esophagitis from other gastrointestinal conditions.
Unlike ulcerative colitis, esophagitis is localized to the esophagus and not the colon.
This class is defined by mucosal injury in the esophagus, not by polyps or resection changes.
normal_cecum: Shows a healthy cecum with no polyps, inflammation, or abnormal findings, unlike pathological classes.
Normal cecum images are distinguished by the presence of anatomical landmarks and absence of disease.
Unlike ulcerative colitis or polyps, the mucosa is smooth and unremarkable.
This class serves as a reference for identifying abnormalities in the cecum.
No dye, lesions, or resection changes are present in normal cecum images.
normal_pylorus: Depicts a healthy pylorus with no evidence of disease, unlike images showing polyps or inflammation.
Normal pylorus is easily distinguished by its regular, round shape and smooth mucosa.
There are no signs of ulceration, mass, or dye application in this class.
Unlike esophagitis or ulcerative colitis, the pylorus is free of inflammatory changes.
This class is unique to the gastric outlet region, with no pathological features.
normal_z_line: Shows a sharp, well-defined squamocolumnar junction, unlike the irregular or inflamed mucosa seen in esophagitis.
Normal Z-line images lack erosions, metaplasia, or dye, setting them apart from diseased classes.
The clear boundary between esophageal and gastric mucosa is a key distinguishing feature.
No polyps, resection margins, or inflammatory changes are present in this class.
This class is specific to the distal esophagus and gastroesophageal junction.
polyps: Characterized by abnormal mucosal growths, polyps are distinct from normal tissue and inflammatory lesions.
Polyps may be sessile or pedunculated, unlike the flat mucosa of normal classes.
Unlike dyed lifted polyps, these are not specifically stained or lifted for enhanced visualization.
Polyps are important to distinguish due to their potential for malignancy, unlike benign normal findings.
This class is defined by the presence of protruding lesions, not by resection or dye application.
ulcerative_colitis: Shows continuous mucosal inflammation and ulceration in the colon, unlike the healthy appearance of normal cecum.
Ulcerative colitis is distinguished by diffuse involvement, as opposed to localized lesions like polyps.
The presence of pseudopolyps, friability, and loss of vascular pattern sets this class apart from others.
Unlike esophagitis, ulcerative colitis affects the colon, not the esophagus.
This class is unique for its chronic, widespread inflammatory changes in the large intestine.
